locked_facts: Faraday's law: EMF = -dΦ/dt (rate of change of flux)
EMF depends on RATE OF CHANGE — not magnitude of flux
Static magnetic field (no change) → zero EMF always
Lenz's law: induced current opposes the change causing it
truth_statements: An EMF is induced in a coil only when the magnetic flux through it changes with time
The magnitude of the induced EMF equals the rate of change of magnetic flux through the coil
Lenz's law states that the induced current flows in a direction that opposes the change in flux that caused it
A constant magnetic field through a stationary coil produces zero EMF — there must be change
The flux can change by changing B, A, or the angle θ between B and the area vector

aha_moment.what_to_show: Show the magnet stationary near the coil — EMF indicator is dark (zero EMF). Then animate the magnet moving: EMF indicator glows brightly. Stop the magnet again — EMF drops to zero. It is the CHANGE that matters, not the presence of the field.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Magnet stationary: zero glow. Magnet moving: bright glow. Magnet stopped again: zero glow. EMF ∝ speed of motion, not field strength.
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students see that a strong static field produces zero EMF — only change produces EMF

simulation_states.label: Coil and bar magnet — setup
simulation_states.description: A coil (turns visible) and a bar magnet positioned nearby. No motion yet. Field lines from magnet visible passing through the coil.
simulation_states.emphasis: A coil of N turns and a bar magnet. Currently stationary — no EMF yet.

simulation_states.label: Magnet moves toward coil — flux increases
simulation_states.description: Magnet begins moving toward the coil. More field lines pass through the coil. EMF indicator starts glowing. Caption: Φ increasing → EMF induced.
simulation_states.emphasis: As the magnet approaches, more flux passes through the coil. dΦ/dt > 0 → EMF is induced!

simulation_states.label: Static field → zero EMF (the key insight)
simulation_states.description: Magnet stops moving. Even though field lines still pass through the coil, the EMF indicator goes dark. Caption: dΦ/dt = 0 → ε = 0. No change, no EMF!
simulation_states.emphasis: The magnet stopped. Field is still there — but EMF is ZERO. No change in flux = no induced EMF. This is the core of Faraday's law.

simulation_states.label: Explore — watch EMF follow motion
simulation_states.description: Animated mode: magnet oscillates back and forth. EMF indicator pulses with each motion. Faster motion = brighter glow. When magnet pauses = dark.
simulation_states.emphasis: Watch: EMF follows the SPEED of the magnet, not its position. Faster = more EMF. Stopped = zero EMF.

trigger_keywords: magnetic field hai toh EMF hoga
magnet near coil gives current always
common_misconceptions.student_belief: If a magnetic field passes through a coil, an EMF is always induced
common_misconceptions.actual_physics: EMF is induced ONLY when the flux CHANGES with time. A strong, constant magnetic field through a coil produces absolutely zero EMF. It is dΦ/dt that matters, not Φ itself.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show magnet stationary near coil with zero EMF, then moving with nonzero EMF. The field is present in both cases — only the change matters.

common_misconceptions.student_belief: The induced EMF is proportional to the amount of magnetic flux
common_misconceptions.actual_physics: The induced EMF is proportional to the RATE OF CHANGE of flux (dΦ/dt), not the flux itself. A small flux changing rapidly produces more EMF than a large flux that is constant.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show fast magnet motion (large dΦ/dt) giving bright EMF vs slow motion (small dΦ/dt) giving dim EMF. Speed of change matters, not amount of field.

typical_question_types: EMF induced in coil with changing B
EMF from rotating coil in uniform field
common_traps: confusing flux with rate of change of flux
forgetting N (number of turns) in EMF formula
assuming constant field means constant EMF
